abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Association master policy and unit owner coverage obligations under the Declaration and Bylaws.
abstracts.subtitle: The Association secures comprehensive coverage for common areas and shared liabilities, while Unit Owners must obtain personal insurance for their units and personal property.

blocks.heading: Association Insurance Policies
blocks.text: The Association maintains broad coverage for Common Elements, Limited Common Elements, and shared liabilities. Premiums are treated as a Common Expense shared by all Unit Owners.

cards.label: Property Insurance
cards.text: All-risk coverage for Common and Limited Common Elements — shared walls, roofs, exteriors, fixtures, and equipment that are part of the building structure.

cards.label: General Liability
cards.text: Minimum $1,000,000 per occurrence for bodily injury, property damage, and personal injury from Association operations or Common Elements.

cards.label: Fidelity Insurance
cards.text: Protects against dishonest acts by Board members, officers, or managers — covers reserve funds and operating accounts.

cards.label: D&O Insurance
cards.text: Covers directors and officers for claims arising from managing the Association.

cards.label: Additional Coverage
cards.text: Flood, earthquake, or other policies as the Board deems necessary based on the property's risk profile.

blocks.heading: Policy Standards
items: Policies must be written by insurers licensed in Washington State with an A.M. Best rating of A- or better.
The Association is the named insured, with Unit Owners and mortgagees as additional insureds.
All policies must include waivers of subrogation to prevent claims against Unit Owners or the Association for covered losses.

blocks.heading: Your Insurance Obligations
blocks.text: You need personal insurance for everything the Association's policy doesn't cover — your unit interior, personal property, and personal liability. Coordinate with the Association to avoid coverage gaps.

cards.label: HO-6 Policy
cards.text: Covers interior walls, fixtures, personal property, improvements, loss of use, and any deductible under the Association's property insurance.

cards.label: Personal Liability
cards.text: Minimum $300,000 per occurrence for bodily injury or property damage caused by you.

cards.label: Loss Assessment
cards.text: Recommended minimum $50,000 — covers assessments levied for uninsured losses or high deductibles on Association policies.

pullQuote.text: If the Association's deductible is $10,000, your HO-6 policy should cover this amount. You are responsible for deductibles when the loss originates from your unit.
pullQuote.caption: Deductible pass-through

action.label: If damage occurs in your unit
action.title: How to handle a claim
steps: Document the damage — photos, dates, and identify the source.
Determine responsibility — if it's your unit interior, your HO-6 applies. If it involves Common Elements, contact the Association.
File with your insurer — you handle unit-specific losses. The Association handles Common Element claims.
Coordinate if both are involved — the Board may require you to submit through the Association's policy first if the loss involves both Common and unit elements.
Provide proof of insurance — the Board may request it at any time.

blocks.heading: Special Conditions
items: Deductibles on the Association's policy may be passed to Unit Owners if the loss originates from their unit.
Both Association and Owner policies must waive subrogation rights.
Policies must comply with mortgage lender requirements, naming mortgagees as additional insureds or loss payees.
The Board reviews insurance coverage annually for adequacy and compliance.

blocks.heading: Enforcement
blocks.text: The Board takes compliance seriously — there are real consequences for not maintaining your required insurance.
items: Owners must provide proof of insurance upon request.
Failure to maintain required insurance may result in fines.
The Association may force-place insurance at the owner's expense.
Unpaid insurance costs may result in a lien on the unit.
The Association maintains records of its policies, available for Owner inspection.

takeaways: The Association carries all-risk property coverage plus $1M minimum liability — premiums are shared as Common Expenses.
You must carry an HO-6 policy, $300,000 minimum personal liability, and $50,000 recommended loss assessment coverage.
You're responsible for Association deductibles when damage originates from your unit.
If you don't maintain insurance, the Association can force-place a policy at your expense and lien your unit.
Both Association and Owner policies must waive subrogation — coordinate coverage to avoid gaps.

abstracts.title: Maintenance Responsibilities
abstracts.desc: Unit owner and Association upkeep obligations, emergency procedures, and enforcement.
abstracts.subtitle: Maintenance duties are split between the Association and Unit Owners, with clear guidelines for routine and emergency repairs, funding, and enforcement.

blocks.heading: What You Maintain
blocks.text: You are responsible for maintaining your unit interior, assigned Limited Common Elements, and any utilities that exclusively serve your unit. All exterior maintenance requires ACC approval.
items: Unit interiors — walls, ceilings, floors, fixtures, appliances, and personal property.
Limited Common Elements assigned to your unit — patios, balconies, assigned parking spaces.
Exterior elements within unit boundaries — windows, doors, and attached fixtures (must meet ACC standards).
Utility systems exclusively serving your unit — plumbing, electrical, including repairs to prevent damage to others.
Prompt repairs — address issues immediately to prevent damage to neighboring units or Common Elements.

action.label: If something needs repair
action.title: How to handle a maintenance issue
steps: Determine responsibility — is it inside your unit or a Common Element? Interior components and unit-exclusive utilities are yours.
If it's yours — arrange repairs. Any exterior work needs ACC approval first.
If it's the Association's — report it to the Property Manager.
If it's urgent and affects others — notify the Association immediately. You may be liable for damage caused by delay.

blocks.heading: What the Association Maintains
blocks.text: The Association handles all Common Elements and shared building components to ensure uniformity and compliance.

cards.label: Structural
cards.text: Shared structural components — roofs, exterior walls, and building systems.

cards.label: Limited Common (Shared)
cards.text: Hallways, exterior lighting, and other Limited Common Elements not assigned to specific units.

cards.label: Exterior Buildings
cards.text: Siding, roofing, painting — all exterior maintenance to ensure uniformity per ACC guidelines.

cards.label: Common Utilities
cards.text: Water mains, electrical systems, and other utilities serving Common Areas.

cards.label: Landscaping
cards.text: Street trees, common area lawns, and irrigation systems.

cards.label: Insurance Repairs
cards.text: Coordination of repairs covered by the Association's property insurance.

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: The Association can act immediately to prevent further damage — entering units without prior notice when necessary.

pullQuote.text: The Association may undertake immediate repairs to Common Elements or units to prevent further damage without prior notice. Costs are charged to the responsible Unit Owner if applicable.
pullQuote.caption: Emergency access authority

items: Owners must address urgent issues in their units immediately (e.g., burst pipes) and notify the Association if Common Elements are affected.
The Association has the right to enter units for emergency repairs with reasonable notice — unless immediate action is required.
Emergency repair costs caused by Owner negligence are charged to that owner, recoverable through assessments or liens.

blocks.heading: How Maintenance Is Funded
blocks.text: Regular assessments cover day-to-day Common Element upkeep. Major repairs come from reserves or special assessments.
items: Common Expenses are funded through regular assessments allocated by ownership percentage.
Special assessments may be levied for major repairs (e.g., roof replacement), approved by majority vote of Owners at a quorum meeting.
The Association maintains a reserve fund for long-term replacement of Common Elements.
Costs for repairs due to Owner negligence may be assessed as a special charge to that Owner.

blocks.heading: What Happens If You Don't Maintain Your Unit
blocks.text: There's a structured enforcement process — it starts with notice and escalates to fines, forced repairs, liens, and legal action.
items: Written notice with a minimum 10-day period to correct the violation.
$50 fine after the grace period, plus $50 every 10 days until compliance.
After 30 days, the Association may hire contractors to fix it at your expense.
Unpaid costs or fines result in a lien on your unit, updated every 6 months until paid.
Persistent non-compliance may lead to small claims court or wage garnishment.

pullQuote.text: $50 fine after 10-day grace period, plus $50 every 10 days. After 30 days, the Association fixes it at your expense. Unpaid amounts become a lien.
pullQuote.caption: Enforcement escalation

takeaways: You maintain your unit interior, assigned Limited Common Elements, and unit-exclusive utilities. The Association handles everything else.
All exterior maintenance or repairs require ACC approval before you start.
The Association can enter your unit for emergencies without notice and charge you if you're responsible.
Fines start at $50 after a 10-day grace period and escalate every 10 days. After 30 days, the Association fixes it and bills you.
Unpaid maintenance costs become a lien on your unit — updated every 6 months until paid.

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.desc: Architectural Control Committee approval process for construction, alterations, and exterior repairs.
abstracts.subtitle: The ACC oversees quality, design, and suitability of improvements to protect property values and neighborhood aesthetics at Summerlin Ridge.

blocks.heading: What Requires ACC Approval?
blocks.text: Any construction, exterior alteration, or repair visible from public streets, Common Areas, or other Units needs prior written approval.
items: New buildings, structures, fences, walls, swimming pools, or additions.
Exterior alterations or repairs — re-roofing, repainting, siding changes.
Landscaping — front yard changes, street tree planting, tree/hedge removal or trimming affecting views or sunlight.
Recreational facilities — pools, tennis courts, or similar significant structures.
Mailboxes — size, color, style, and location.
Antenna or satellite dishes (except dishes 24 inches or less, subject to Board rules).
Fences — particularly those over 3 feet or between the house and roadway.
Excavations — any dirt movement unless necessary for approved improvements.

action.label: Before you start any project
action.title: ACC approval step by step
steps: Submit duplicate plans at least 15 days before — include location, materials, colors, and design details.
ACC reviews within 5 days — evaluates quality, conformity with existing structures, and compliance with setbacks.
Written approval required — if the ACC doesn't respond within 15 days, the submission is deemed approved.
Professional input may be required — the ACC can require plans by an approved architect and construction by an approved contractor.
Complete exterior appearance within 12 months — painting, landscaping, and exterior finish must be done within 12 months of construction start.

pullQuote.text: If the ACC does not respond within 15 days, the submission is deemed approved.
pullQuote.caption: Auto-approval provision

blocks.heading: Materials & Design Standards
items: All improvements must comply with applicable zoning, building, and environmental regulations.
Roofing must be 20-year composition material in a consistent color.
Siding must be horizontal lap or vertical channel; brick, stucco, or vinyl allowed at ACC discretion. T-111 is prohibited.
Exterior colors require ACC approval.
Driveways must be concrete, asphalt, or ACC-approved materials.

blocks.heading: Fences & Landscaping
items: No fences between the house and roadway right-of-way except decorative fences up to 3 feet.
All fences must be wood.
Front yard must include at least one street tree matching existing species and caliber.
The ACC considers impact on views, sunlight, and noise for nearby properties.

blocks.heading: Non-Compliance
blocks.text: Violations trigger a structured enforcement process with escalating consequences.
items: Written notice with a minimum 10-day period to correct the violation.
$50 fine after the grace period, plus $50 every 10 days until compliance.
After 30 days, the HOA may hire a company to fix it at your expense.
Unpaid fines or costs result in a lien on the property, updated every 6 months.
Persistent non-compliance may lead to small claims court or wage garnishment.

takeaways: All exterior changes, construction, or repairs require prior ACC approval.
Submit duplicate plans at least 15 days before starting — the ACC reviews within 5 days.
No response within 15 days = deemed approved.
Exterior appearance must be completed within 12 months of construction start.
Non-compliance starts at $50 per 10-day period and can escalate to liens and court action.

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Governance structure, elections, authority, and current board composition.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors manages rules, finances, maintenance, and legal matters for Summerlin Ridge. Directors serve without compensation, acting in the best interest of all Unit Owners.

cards.label: Board Size
cards.text: 3 to 7 directors (2 during Declarant control). Adjustable by Bylaw amendment.

cards.label: Qualifications
cards.text: Must be natural persons and Washington residents. A majority must be Unit Owners.

cards.label: Terms
cards.text: 1-year terms. Initial directors serve 2 years for staggered transition.

blocks.heading: Transition to Owner Control
blocks.text: As units are sold to non-Declarant owners, the Board progressively transitions to elected representation.
items: At 25% sold — at least 25% of Board members elected by Unit Owners.
At 50% sold — at least 33⅓% of Board members elected by Unit Owners.
After Declarant control — Owners elect a Board of at least 3, with a majority being Unit Owners.

blocks.heading: Election Process
blocks.text: Elections use mailed ballots requiring a 60% member quorum. No cumulative voting is allowed.

pullQuote.text: Unit Owners can remove a director (except Declarant-appointed) with a ⅔ vote of voting power at a quorum meeting. Directors can also remove a colleague with a ⅔ vote of directors in office.
pullQuote.caption: Removal thresholds

blocks.heading: Meetings & Decision-Making
items: Annual Board meeting follows director elections; regular meetings set by Board resolution.
Special meetings called by the President or a majority of directors.
Meetings may occur via conference call — all participants must be able to hear each other.
72 hours' notice required for non-regular meetings; attendance waives notice.
Majority of directors constitutes a quorum; majority vote of those present is binding.
Actions may be taken without a meeting if all directors sign written consent.
Public positions or borrowing require a ⅔ vote of directors in office.

blocks.heading: Officers of the Board
blocks.text: Officers are elected annually by the Board, must be Washington residents, and President and Secretary cannot be the same person.

cards.label: President
cards.text: Chief executive — presides over meetings, appoints committees, ensures Board actions are carried out.

cards.label: Secretary
cards.text: Keeps minutes and records, maintains member lists.

cards.label: Treasurer
cards.text: Manages finances, oversees audits, prepares budgets and reports.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
blocks.text: Directors serve without compensation but may be reimbursed for Board-approved expenses. Directors and officers are indemnified to the fullest extent under Washington law. The Association may maintain insurance to cover indemnification obligations.

blocks.heading: What the Board Can Do
blocks.text: The Board has comprehensive authority but delegates tasks through committees and agents while retaining control over major decisions.
items: Manage Association affairs, enforce rules, and oversee maintenance.
Adopt and amend Bylaws, rules, and budgets.
Appoint officers and establish committees (including the ACC).
Engage managers or agents for delegated duties.
Enforce rules per the Declaration with fines and corrective action.

blocks.heading: Committees
items: Executive Committee — exercises Board authority except for major decisions (borrowing, Bylaw amendments).
Nominating Committee — proposes director and officer nominees annually.
Functional Committees — formed for specific tasks, chaired by a director.

blocks.heading: Amendments & Deadlocks
blocks.text: The Board can amend, alter, or repeal Bylaws and adopt new ones. Arbitration resolves Board voting deadlocks, with an arbitrator chosen unanimously or by the Snohomish County Superior Court.

takeaways: 3–7 directors serve 1-year terms. A majority must be Unit Owners after Declarant control ends.
Directors can be removed by ⅔ vote of Owners or ⅔ vote of fellow directors.
Elections require 60% member quorum via mailed ballots — no cumulative voting.
Public positions or borrowing require ⅔ vote of directors in office.
Directors serve without pay but are fully indemnified under Washington law.
Arbitration resolves Board deadlocks — Snohomish County Superior Court appoints arbitrator if needed.

abstracts.title: Resale Certificate
abstracts.desc: Legally mandated disclosures required for the sale or transfer of any unit.
abstracts.subtitle: A Resale Certificate is required for every unit sale at Summerlin Ridge, providing buyers with critical financial, governance, and operational disclosures.

blocks.heading: Why It Exists
blocks.text: Washington law (RCW 64.34.425) requires a Resale Certificate for all unit sales or transfers (except exemptions like foreclosures or gifts). It informs buyers about the Association's financial health, governance, and obligations — enabling informed purchase decisions. It must be delivered before closing.

blocks.heading: Who Prepares It?
blocks.text: The Board or its designated agent (e.g., management company) prepares the certificate upon written request from the selling Owner. The Secretary ensures preparation, with financial input from the Treasurer.

action.label: If you're selling your unit
action.title: How to get your Resale Certificate
steps: Request in writing — contact the Board or Property Manager.
Pay the preparation fee — the seller is responsible for all costs.
Association responds within 10 days — this is a legal requirement under RCW 64.34.425.
Clear any outstanding obligations — assessments, fines, or violations must be resolved before closing.
Deliver to the buyer before closing — the buyer has 5 days to cancel after receiving it.

pullQuote.text: The Association must provide the Resale Certificate within 10 days of the request. Failure to comply violates RCW 64.34.425 and may delay the sale.
pullQuote.caption: 10-day response requirement

blocks.heading: Financial Disclosures
items: Current assessment amount and payment schedule.
Unpaid assessments, fines, or special assessments owed by the selling unit.
Current budget and reserve study, including reserve fund balance.
Anticipated special assessments or changes in regular assessments.

blocks.heading: Governance & Legal
items: Copies or summaries of Declaration, Bylaws, Articles, and Rules.
Pending or threatened litigation involving the Association.
Names and contact info of current Board members and management company.
Statement of compliance with RCW 64.34.425, certified by an officer.

blocks.heading: Property & Insurance
items: Details of Association property, liability, fidelity, and D&O insurance — coverage limits and deductibles.
Reports on condition of Common Elements, including known defects or required repairs.
Restrictions on unit use, occupancy, or alterations and any violations by the selling unit.
Transfer fees and the Association's lien rights for unpaid amounts.

pullQuote.text: Buyers have 5 days after receiving the Resale Certificate to cancel without penalty. Cancellation must be in writing. The buyer gets a full refund of any deposit.
pullQuote.caption: Buyer cancellation right — RCW 64.34.425(3)

blocks.heading: Seller Obligations
blocks.text: The seller must request the certificate, pay preparation fees, and ensure all outstanding assessments or fines are cleared before closing. The Association may place a lien for unpaid amounts that must be resolved before transfer.

blocks.heading: Why Compliance Matters
items: Failure to provide a complete certificate within 10 days violates Washington law and may delay the sale.
Sellers who fail to disclose known issues may face legal action from buyers or the Association.
The Board may withhold sale approval if the unit is non-compliant (e.g., unapproved alterations).
The Association maintains records of all certificates issued, available for Owner inspection.

takeaways: A Resale Certificate is mandatory for every unit sale — required by Washington law.
The Association must respond within 10 days. The seller pays all preparation fees.
It includes financial, governance, insurance, property condition, and compliance disclosures.
Buyers have 5 days to cancel after receiving the certificate — with full deposit refund.
Clear all outstanding assessments and violations before closing to avoid liens or delays.
The Board can withhold sale approval if unapproved alterations or violations exist.

abstracts.title: Quick Reference
abstracts.desc: Key numbers, deadlines, and thresholds from all governing documents in one place.
abstracts.subtitle: Every important number, deadline, and threshold from across all Summerlin Ridge governing document abstracts.

cards.label: ACC Submission
cards.text: Submit duplicate plans at least 15 days before starting.

cards.label: ACC Review
cards.text: ACC reviews within 5 days. No response in 15 days = deemed approved.

cards.label: Exterior Completion
cards.text: Exterior appearance must be finished within 12 months of construction start.

cards.label: Violation Grace
cards.text: 10-day period to correct after written notice.

cards.label: Resale Response
cards.text: Association must provide Resale Certificate within 10 days.

cards.label: Buyer Cancellation
cards.text: 5 days to cancel after receiving Resale Certificate.

cards.label: Initial Fine
cards.text: $50 after 10-day grace period.

cards.label: Recurring Fine
cards.text: $50 every 10 days until compliance.

cards.label: Forced Repair
cards.text: After 30 days, the HOA fixes it at your expense.

cards.label: Liens
cards.text: Unpaid fines or costs become a lien, updated every 6 months.

cards.label: Court Action
cards.text: Small claims court or wage garnishment for persistent non-payment.

cards.label: Remove Director
cards.text: ⅔ vote of voting power at a quorum meeting (or ⅔ of directors in office).

cards.label: Election Quorum
cards.text: 60% member quorum required for elections via mailed ballot.

cards.label: Board Quorum
cards.text: Majority of directors present.

cards.label: Public Positions / Borrowing
cards.text: ⅔ vote of directors in office.

cards.label: Special Assessments
cards.text: Majority vote of Owners at a quorum meeting.

cards.label: Association Liability
cards.text: $1,000,000 minimum per occurrence.

cards.label: Insurer Rating
cards.text: A.M. Best A- or better, licensed in Washington.

cards.label: Owner Liability
cards.text: $300,000 minimum personal liability.

cards.label: Loss Assessment
cards.text: $50,000 recommended minimum.

cards.label: Force-Placed
cards.text: Association may force-place insurance at owner's expense.

takeaways: Submit ACC plans 15 days early — auto-approved if no response in 15 days.
Fines start at $50 per 10-day period. After 30 days, the HOA fixes it and bills you.
Carry $300,000 liability and $50,000 loss assessment coverage — or the Association can force-place at your cost.
Directors can be removed by ⅔ vote. Elections require 60% quorum.
The Association must deliver a Resale Certificate within 10 days. Buyers get 5 days to cancel.
Exterior appearance must be completed within 12 months of construction start.
